content.problemStatement:
Bugs Bunny is very smart, but he keeps constantly worrying about the future and is always anxious. Therefore, he keeps hopping between towns without standing still.

The place where Bugs Bunny lives has NNN towns (numbered 111 through NNN) with one-way roads connecting them in such a way that they form an arborescence (a tree with all paths directed from the root), rooted at town 111. Therefore, each town except the root has exactly one incoming road; for each iii (1≤i≤N−11 \le i \le N-11≤i≤N−1), there is a road from town PiP_iPi​ to town i+1i+1i+1. Each town has a distinct rating associated with it; for each valid iii, the iii-th town has rating AiA_iAi​.

From a town iii, Bugs Bunny is allowed to hop to town jjj (without visiting any other towns in between), if the following conditions are satisfied:


there is a path between towns iii and jjj, i.e. a path from town iii to town jjj or from town jjj to town iii
town jjj has a lower rating than town iii, i.e. Aj<AiA_j \lt A_iAj​<Ai​
This way, Bugs Bunny can visit a sequence of towns by hopping, starting anywhere and stopping whenever he chooses. Clearly, he cannot keep hopping forever, so the number of such sequences is finite.

Tell Bugs Bunny the number of sequences of two or more towns which can be formed by hopping, given that he can start at any town and stop at any town. Since this number can be very large, calculate it modulo 109+710^9+7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains N−1N-1N−1 space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PN−1P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_{N-1}P1​,P2​,…,PN−1​.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing one integer --- the number of valid sequences of visited towns modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The given edges form a rooted directed tree (arborescence) with root 1
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer N, the number of towns.
- The second line contains N‑1 integers P1 … P_{N‑1}, where Pi is the parent of town i+1 (the tree is rooted at 1).
- The third line contains N distinct integers A1 … AN, the rating of each town.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the number of valid sequences of length at least two, modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The possible hops for Bugs Bunny are from town 111 to town 333 and from town 222 to town 111. Therefore, the 333 possible sequences of visited towns are (2,1,3)(2,1,3)(2,1,3), (2,1)(2,1)(2,1) and (1,3)(1,3)(1,3).

solution.approach:
The rating is strictly decreasing along every allowed hop, therefore the directed graph formed by all possible hops is a DAG.  For a node u let dp[u] be the number of valid sequences that start at u (the first town is u, the sequence contains at least one hop).  When we are at u we may jump to any already‑processed node v that lies on the same root‑to‑leaf path (ancestor or descendant) and has a lower rating.  For each such v we can either stop after the hop (contributing 1) or continue with any sequence that starts at v (contributing dp[v]).  Hence
    dp[u] = Σ_{v reachable, rating(v)<rating(u)} (1 + dp[v]).
Processing towns in decreasing order of rating guarantees that every v in the sum has already been processed.
We need to obtain, for the current node u, the sum of (1+dp) over all processed descendants of u and over all processed ancestors of u.  Using an Euler tour we map each subtree to a contiguous interval [tin, tout].
Two Fenwick trees are maintained:
1. BIT1 – point add, range sum.  When a node v is processed we add (1+dp[v]) at position tin[v]; a range query on [tin[u], tout[u]] gives the sum over processed descendants of u.
2. BIT2 – range add, point query.  When a node v is processed we add (1+dp[v]) to the whole interval of its subtree; a point query at tin[u] then yields the sum over processed ancestors of u.
The algorithm for each test case:
- Build the tree, run a DFS to obtain tin/tout.
- Sort nodes by rating descending.
- Initialise both BITs with zeros.
- Iterate nodes in that order, compute dp[u] = (BIT1.rangeSum(tin[u], tout[u]) + BIT2.pointQuery(tin[u])) mod M.
- Add dp[u] to the global answer.
- Let val = (1 + dp[u]) mod M and update BIT1.add(tin[u], val) and BIT2.rangeAdd(tin[u], tout[u], val).
The answer is the sum of dp[u] for all nodes.
Complexities: O(N log N) time per test case, O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting + Fenwick operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the tree, Euler tour arrays and two Fenwick trees.
